architecture.summary: Atlas is a retrieval-augmented sequence-to-sequence model consisting of two jointly-trained components: a dense retriever (based on Contriever, using dual-encoder architecture with average pooling) that retrieves top-k documents from a large corpus, and a language model (T5 with Fusion-in-Decoder) that processes retrieved documents independently in the encoder, concatenates their representations, and performs cross-attention decoding. Information flows from query → retriever embeddings → top-k document retrieval → language model encoder (processing each document with query prepended) → concatenated encoder outputs → decoder with cross-attention → text output.

biblio.title: Atlas: Few-shot Learning with Retrieval Augmented Language Models

claims.statement: Atlas reaches over 42% accuracy on Natural Questions using only 64 examples, outperforming a 540B parameters model by 3% despite having 50x fewer parameters
Atlas achieves 64.0% accuracy in full-dataset setting with a Wikipedia index on NaturalQuestions, establishing a new state of the art by 8 points
On 64-shot KILT tasks, Atlas with Perplexity Distillation achieves 45.0% exact match on NaturalQuestions, 15.0% F1 on Wizard of Wikipedia, and 77.0% accuracy on FEVER
Jointly pre-training the retriever and language model is crucial for few-shot performance, with models using retrieval during pre-training strongly outperforming models without joint pre-training

Among retriever training objectives (Attention Distillation, EMDR2, Perplexity Distillation, Leave-one-out Perplexity Distillation), no significant systematic differences are observed between methods, with Perplexity Distillation adopted for subsequent experiments due to stability and computational efficiency
Query-side fine-tuning (fixing document encoder parameters) avoids index refresh overhead with minimal performance impact in few-shot settings
Index refresh overhead during pre-training is around 30% compared to training with a fixed retriever, when refreshing every 1,000 steps out of 10,000 total pre-training iterations
Masked language modeling and prefix language modeling pretext tasks both achieve comparable few-shot performance, with masked language modeling slightly preferred (42.4% vs 40.1% average on 64-shot)
